articles.headline: J. M. M'Kim's letter from Washington -- visit to W. H. Williams' slave pen
articles.author: J. M. M'Kim
articles.summary: Letter dated Washington, Feb. 6, 1838, describing the writer's journey from Harrisburg through Baltimore to Washington, witnessing a coffle of slaves (including a family separated -- mother, father, and children -- being loaded into a rail car bound for Alabama) and a young slaveholder's cruelty toward his own slave; the writer then visits W. H. Williams' slave factory on 7th Street between Pennsylvania and Maryland avenues, is shown the holding pen and cellar quarters by Williams' agent (Williams himself being in Natchez), and describes the daily rations, cramped sleeping arrangements, and the business of holding slaves for other owners at a fee. Likely James Miller M'Kim, later a prominent Pennsylvania abolitionist.
articles.notes: Rare firsthand eyewitness account of the domestic slave trade infrastructure in Washington, D.C.

articles.headline: Letter to Rev. S. E. Cornish -- William Whipper on the American Moral Reform Society constitution
articles.author: William Whipper
articles.summary: Dated Columbia, Feb. 12, 1838. Whipper argues that the American Moral Reform Society's constitution should not be read as confining its work to colored people specifically, proposing three race-neutral propositions (citizenship, national moral character, and support of Education/Temperance/Economy/Universal Liberty) as the only legitimate questions in the ongoing controversy with editor Cornish over whether the Society should organize on a race-specific or universal basis.
articles.notes: First installment of a multi-issue public debate between Whipper and Cornish over the AMRS's founding principles, continued in CA_1838-03-29.

articles.headline: Lectures of the Philomathean Society -- 1838 course schedule
articles.summary: Full schedule of the New York Philomathean Society's 1838 lecture course: Chemistry by Dr. John Brown (3 lectures), Organs of Sense by J. McCune Smith M.D. (4), Oratorical Delivery by Mr. R. P. Wake (4), Evidences of Christianity by I. G. DeGrasse A.B. (5), History by P. A. Bell (4), Geography by Mr. J. Peterson (4), Logic by Mr. S. V. Berry (2), Decision of Character by Mr. James Field (2). Committee on Lectures: P. A. Bell, L. V. Clarke, Eli Hazzard.

articles.headline: A Pleasing Fact -- revivals at Zion's Methodist Episcopal Church and Frankfort Street Presbyterian Church
articles.summary: Report of ongoing revival meetings at Zion's Methodist Episcopal Church under Rev. Jacob D. Richardson (over 100 seeking reconciliation, sixty already joined the church) and at the Frankfort Street Presbyterian Church under Rev. Theodore S. Wright.

articles.headline: Right of Suffrage -- reprint of 1821 New York Convention speeches
articles.summary: Excerpts from Yates' 'Rights of Colored Men' reprinting speeches from the 1821 New York constitutional convention debate on Black suffrage: Abraham Van Vechten arguing against disfranchisement on interstate-privileges grounds, Peter A. Jay's rebuttal that power and right are not equivalent, and Dr. Robert Clarke's speech invoking the three-fifths apportionment clause and military service by men of color in the Revolution and War of 1812.

articles.headline: Swindling -- Phoebe vs. Johnson, a Cincinnati case exposing the ban on Black testimony
articles.summary: A colored woman named Phoebe was cheated of a $50 note by a wagoner named Johnson in Cincinnati; because Ohio law barred her testimony against a white man, a magistrate (Richard Ayres) could not issue a warrant until a white witness was found. A constable recovered the money by tricking Johnson into believing a white girl had witnessed the theft. Lawyer named as Mr. Piatt.

articles.headline: Great and Important Meeting -- Philadelphia response to the Reform Convention
articles.summary: Reprinted from the Pennsylvania Freeman: a highly important meeting of colored citizens of Philadelphia at the First Presbyterian Church, Seventh Street, to consider the Reform Convention's disfranchisement move. John P. Burr chosen President; Thomas Butler and S. H. Gloucester Vice Presidents; James Cornish and James Forten Jr. Secretaries. Robert Purvis of Bucks County read the 'appeal of 40,000 disenfranchised citizens of Pennsylvania.' Remarks by James Forten, Robert Purvis, J. C. Bowers, F. A. Hinton, and Charles Gardner; the appeal adopted unanimously.

articles.headline: Postscript -- kidnapping of Henry Robertson
articles.author: W. P. Johnson
articles.summary: A free man named Henry Robertson was seized from his own doorstep by a carriage party before he had left his house that morning, on a claim of being the property of a man named Nash; despite his free papers, he was carried off within minutes. Signed W. P. Johnson.

articles.headline: Toronto colored inhabitants' meeting on the Solomon Moseby case
articles.summary: Meeting of colored inhabitants of Toronto, Jan. 13, 1838, at the residence of Mr. Wm. Osborne; Stephen Dutton chosen chair, Wm. Augustus secretary. Resolutions (moved/seconded by M. B. Trust, H. Coles, John L. Randolph, Wm. Hickman, Samuel Thompson, George Brown, Richard Burke, George Williams, James C. Brown, George Wilkinson) condemn the near-lynching of Solomon Moseby, a self-emancipated slave, at Niagara the previous September, and affirm gratitude for British anti-slavery efforts and confidence in Canadian legal protection for refugees.

articles.headline: Continuing debate -- William Whipper's second letter to Cornish on the American Moral Reform Society
articles.author: William Whipper
articles.summary: Dated Columbia, March 19, 1838. Whipper declines to discuss Cornish's counter-propositions as illegitimate, restates his universalist position that the Society's constitution should not be confined by complexional distinctions, and lays out an extended argument that moral duties and citizenship are not properly divided by race; Cornish's editorial rebuttal follows, defending 'special efforts' for the colored population specifically and praising Augustine's letters as the best writing yet on the duty and policy of colored men.

articles.headline: Kidnapping/rescue of George Stewart from Baton Rouge imprisonment
articles.author: P. A. Bell
articles.summary: Letter to the editor from P. A. Bell, March 26, 1838, reporting that George Stewart, a native of New York City, had been imprisoned in Baton Rouge on a charge of being a slave, and that efforts (through correspondents Mr. Satchel of Cincinnati) succeeded in procuring his liberation; he is expected home shortly to thank those who assisted.

articles.headline: Death of Mrs. Lucia Ann Kent
articles.summary: Notice, reprinted from the Liberator, of the death of Lucia Ann Kent, wife of George Kent of Concord, N.H., aged 30, described as a zealous, intrepid anti-slavery worker; grouped with the losses of Elizabeth M. Chandler and Anne G. Chapman as afflictive to the movement.

articles.headline: Ohio Memorial extracts -- self-purchased farmers of Brown, Dark, and other counties
articles.summary: Continuing extracts from the Ohio colored citizens' memorial, naming formerly enslaved men who purchased their own freedom and became substantial landowners: Isaac Holland (160 acres), Ezekiel Lewis (200 acres), Thornton Alexander (520 acres, freed at 36, property valued at $15,000), James Clemens, and Brown County's Samuel Jones ($500 for himself), Basil Dover ($1,700 for himself and family), David Rees ($500), and Charles Black (property valued at $6,000); testimonial from neighbor George P. Cowgill vouching for their honesty and fitness to vote and testify in court.

articles.headline: Memorial of the People of Color of Philadelphia to the Reform Convention
articles.author: John C. Bowers
articles.summary: Reprint of the June 5, 1837 memorial from an overflowing meeting of Philadelphia's people of color at Bethel Church, protesting proposed restrictions on Black emigration into Pennsylvania and the threatened loss of suffrage; signed John C. Bowers, Chairman of the Committee; James Cornish and Morris Brown, Chairmen of the meeting; James Needham, Secretary.

articles.headline: J. H. Kimball's valedictory retirement from the Herald of Freedom
articles.author: J. H. Kimball
articles.summary: J. H. Kimball, co-author of 'Emancipation in the West Indies,' announces his retirement as editor of the Herald of Freedom due to failing health, reaffirming his lifelong commitment to abolitionism in a farewell address reprinted with commentary praising his service.

articles.headline: James Fields' speech on the Lovejoy memorial resolution
articles.author: James Fields
articles.summary: Speech by Mr. James Fields responding to the charge that colored Americans are ungrateful to abolitionists, delivered on a resolution of sympathy for the widow and orphan of the Rev. Elijah P. Lovejoy; affirms deep gratitude of the colored community toward abolitionists who have risked persecution on their behalf.

articles.headline: Ohio Memorial -- Godfrey's manumission and family purchase documents (Surry/Brunswick Co., Virginia)
articles.summary: Full transcription of the 1814-1816 legal papers by which a shoemaker named Godfrey (formerly enslaved in Surry County, Virginia, owned by John T. Bowdoin) purchased his own freedom for $400 and then purchased his wife China and their seven children (Sam, Bibanna, Dick, Godfrey, Miles, Elizabeth, Polly) plus two older children (Sally and Moses) already bought earlier, for a total exceeding $1,700; also recounts George Moss of Warren County (freed at 35, paid $550 for himself and $400 for his wife) and Anthony Barcor (freed at 40 for $666, then purchased his wife and three children for $550), with Barcor's 1832 character certificate signed by fifty inhabitants of Fredericksburg, Virginia.

articles.headline: J. B. Vashon -- Right of Suffrage in Pennsylvania (Pittsburgh)
articles.author: J. B. Vashon
articles.summary: Letter dated Pittsburgh, March 26, 1838, from John B. Vashon responding to the Colored American's editorial on Black political apathy, noting that in Pittsburgh colored men have long voted as a matter of course and were courted by all parties, and that four of the county's five delegates to the Reform Convention voted to retain the right of suffrage as a result.

articles.headline: Examination of the New York Colored Free Schools
articles.author: Chester
articles.summary: Correspondent 'Chester' describes the public examination of the New York African Free Schools (founded by the Manumission Society), held before a crowd of 350-400 pupils and observers. A trustee (also Recording Secretary of the New York Colonization Society) ordered colored women off the platform reserved for trustees and white attendees -- an incident the writer denounces as caste prejudice intruding even into a school for colored children. Teachers named: Mr. Rand and Dr. Brown (School No. 1, boys); Miss Caroline and Elizabeth Roe (School No. 1, girls); Mr. Peterson and Miss Tompkins (Nos. 4 and 6). Samuel Seaton Esq. addressed the assembly, followed by Mr. Holbrook of Philadelphia (showing bird drawings from a school in Africa), Rev. Theodore S. Wright, and Lindley Murray.

articles.headline: Primus Babcock -- Revolutionary War veteran's discharge signed by George Washington
articles.summary: From Yates' 'Rights of Colored Men,' via a speech of Calvin Goddard Esq.: an aged colored Revolutionary War veteran, Primus Babcock, presented an honorable discharge dated at the close of the war, written entirely in George Washington's own hand, in support of a pension application; cited as proof of Black citizenship and military service.

articles.headline: New York Legislature debate on Texas annexation and Patton's gag resolution
articles.summary: Report of an April 1838 New York Assembly debate over resolutions protesting the admission of Texas and Mr. Patton's resolution to table anti-slavery petitions without reading; speakers include Messrs. Mann, Culver, Denniston, Silliman, Hulbert, Wardwell, P. King, and Taylor, with references to Gerrit Smith's abolitionist rhetoric being disputed on the floor. Resolutions protesting Texas's admission passed 57-9.

events.name: Great and Important Meeting -- Philadelphia colored citizens respond to the Reform Convention
events.location: First Presbyterian Church, Seventh Street, Philadelphia
events.description: Public meeting of colored citizens of Philadelphia to adopt an appeal of 40,000 disenfranchised citizens of Pennsylvania against the Reform Convention's move to restrict suffrage to whites; appeal read by Robert Purvis and adopted unanimously.
attendees: read the appeal, of Bucks County

events.name: Toronto colored inhabitants' meeting on the Solomon Moseby case
events.location: residence of Mr. Wm. Osborne, Toronto, Upper Canada
events.description: Meeting of colored inhabitants of Toronto condemning the attempted re-enslavement and near-lynching of Solomon Moseby, a self-emancipated slave, at Niagara in September 1837, and affirming confidence in British/Canadian legal protection for refugees from slavery.

events.name: Examination of the New York Colored Free Schools
events.location: New York African Free Schools (East District, Nos. 1, 4, 6)
events.description: Public examination of the New York Manumission Society's Colored Free Schools before an audience of 350-400; incident of racial exclusion from the trustees' platform noted and denounced; addresses by several community figures followed the exercises.

events.name: Revival meetings at Zion's Methodist Episcopal Church and Frankfort Street Presbyterian Church
events.description: Series of revival meetings reported at two New York churches, with over a hundred individuals at Zion's Church seeking reconciliation and sixty already having joined the church.
